answer:
1. Compounding & Morphemic Structure: Maonan forms words by combining morphemes (e.g., adjective-noun `d…ô.ma` (big pig), verb-adverb `put.p…îk` (to spray resolutely), complex nouns like `ni.gwi.dak` (elephant)).
2. Modifier Order: Adjectives can precede (`d…ô.ma` - big pig) or follow (`n…îk.?…õp la:n` - bad chicken, `nam.nda` - red mallard) nouns. Verb adverbs follow verbs (`put.p…îk`).
3. Homonymy: Some morphemes appear homonymous (e.g., `nam` (mallard) vs `nam` (sprayer-tool suffix); `na` (tear) vs `na` (wearer)). Vowel length (e.g., `nam` vs `na:m`) distinguishes other words.
4. Specific Gender Markers: Female markers are species-specific (`ka:i` for chickens, `ni` for pigs) rather than general.
5. Idiomatic Expressions: Some phrases have meanings not directly compositional from their morphemes (e.g., `d…ô.ka:i na:m` (bull), `dat.tan` (to eat resolutely), `da:i.na` (clothing)).
